text.content:
Settings live in Game Settings > Module Settings > Ionrift Respite. Each setting carries its own hint, so this page sticks to the things the panel cannot show in a hover.

Starting a rest

Only the GM starts a rest. Three ways:

  • Type /respite in chat.
  • Click the campground icon in the token controls on the left of the canvas.
  • Let a player click Long Rest on their character sheet. With Intercept Player Rests on (default), the Respite flow opens instead of the native dialog.

Quick Setup profiles

The top of the settings panel offers Simple, Standard, and Survival presets. Pick the closest one, then tune in the panels below. Standard is the recommended starting point: structured camp with professions, training, fletching, and night encounters, but no comfort tiers, meals, or scouting. Survival adds comfort tiers, meal tracking, and travel scouting.

Training

Off under Simple. On under Standard and Survival at the default tier. Set the rate under Rest Activities; slide to Off to remove the activity entirely.

Training targets characters level 5 and below who are close to their next level. The intent is a rest-time nudge over the threshold, not a replacement for session XP. The activity card highlights when someone is within reach of leveling and a training rest could close the gap.

Each pick runs three checks (highest ability modifier vs DC 13, comfort-adjusted when comfort rules are on). Every set pays XP on pass or fail. Diminishing returns: each consecutive long rest spent training withholds XP on an escalating scale (15 XP after one prior training rest at the default tier; 45 XP after two). The streak resets when the character chooses a different activity. Two back-to-back training rests pay far less combined than two training rests spaced across other camp activities. The card marks training non-viable once the payout would hit zero.

See the Training page in this compendium for the XP tier table and slider settings.

Comfort, in detail

Off under Simple and Standard. Turn on with the Survival Quick Setup profile, or toggle Comfort Rules under Recovery Rules.

When comfort is on, it is the headline mechanic. The GM sets the baseline from the environment. Shelter, fire, gear, and certain camp activities shift it up or down. The tier the rest resolves at determines:

TierHP recoveryHit Dice penaltyExhaustion saveEncounter pressure
SafeFullNoneNoneNone (skipped)
ShelteredFullNoneNoneNormal
RoughFull−1 HDCON DC 10 or +1 exhaustionRaised
Hostile75% of max−2 HDCON DC 15 or +1 exhaustion; existing exhaustion cannot clearHigh

The comfort badge anywhere in the UI shows the same hint on hover. When comfort is off, recovery uses a flat pass with no tier penalties, no fire phase, and no terrain exhaustion saves.

Travel scouting

Off by default (Standard and Simple). Turn on under Rest Activities > Travel Scouting, or apply the Survival Quick Setup profile. Requires the travel phase (Crafting Professions on). Runs on the final travel day only. Suppressed at a safe rest spot.

Each scout rolls Perception or Survival (higher modifier on the sheet). There is no fixed DC. The d20 total maps to a tier. Multiple scouts may roll; only the highest total applies. Results are GM-only. The travel debrief panel supplies terrain flavor lines to read aloud.

TierTotalComfortNight checkOther
Nat 1≤ 1——Hidden complication. Describe the site as good; a negative scouting event injects during the Events phase.
Poor2–9——Narrative only.
Average10–14+1 tier——
Good15+ (not nat 20)+1 tier−1 Encounter DCEasier quiet night.
Nat 2015+ with d20 = 20+2 tiers—Positive scouting event injects during Events (independent of the night d20).

Scouting modifiers stack into the encounter bar the same way as shelter and weather. See the night-check breakdown during Events.

Terrains and events

Terrain sets the event pool and the baseline encounter DC. Each ships with its own pool: forest, desert, swamp, coast, arctic, underdark, urban, ruins, dungeon, tavern.

Events resolve on a four-tier outcome scale (triumph, success, mixed, failure) shaped by how far the GM's roll lands above or below the night's DC. Categories: Encounter (something hostile), Complication (a problem to solve), Discovery (something useful), Ambient (colour, no mechanics).

Curating the pool

Camp events only fire if you have added them to the active pool. Open Module Settings > Ionrift Respite > Curate Event Pool, or click Curate Pool from the Content Packs panel. Until the pool has entries, a low night-check roll simply passes with no event, so you can read through everything before anything can surprise the table.

Meals and diets

Off by default. Turn it on under Food & Diet if you want rations and water tracked at camp. Per-character dietary rules and preset profiles live in the same dialog.

If a rest gets stuck

Reset Rest State in the settings panel (under Tools) clears any lock and removes camp tokens from the active scene. Use it when a rest cannot start, the party needs to rest again the same in-game day, or a previous rest did not finish cleanly.
